Eleanor stood before the massive double doors carved from black iron. The wind howled past the window, a sound like distant trains rushing through a tunnel. She turned the key, expecting the mundane click of a lock. Instead, the metal hummed with a frequency that made her teeth ache.

Pushing them apart revealed a corridor stretching infinitely long. Paintings lined the walls, yet the faces upon them changed. First came a man in tweed smoking a pipe; next, a woman in a space suit looking up at stars. The air grew cold, then hot, then humid.

She stepped across the threshold. Her first breath tasted of coal smoke and coal dust. Steam engines puffed in the distance. It was 1895. She ran up the stairs. The carpet felt brittle, worn by decades of heels. Down in the cellar, the silence was absolute. Monitors flickered on a desk. Blueprints covered the floor. It was the year 2088.

The house did not merely store time; it consumed and reshaped it. The walls breathed. Doors opened to mornings that had passed centuries ago or futures yet unwritten.

Eleanor leaned against a doorframe, watching the shadow stretch across the floorboards. She knew the answer lay not in the past, but in the control the structure exerted over existence. She closed her eyes, listening to the creak of timber settling into the new age.

She stepped back from the threshold, accepting that time bends to the will of the house.<|im_end|>

Eleanor stood before the massive double doors carved from black iron. The wind howled past the window, a sound like distant trains rushing through a tunnel. She turned the key, expecting the mundane click of a lock. Instead, the metal hummed with a frequency that made her teeth ache.

Pushing them apart revealed a corridor stretching infinitely long. Paintings lined the walls, yet the faces upon them changed. First came a man in tweed smoking a pipe; next, a woman in a space suit looking up at stars. The air grew cold, then hot, then humid.

She stepped across the threshold. Her first breath tasted of coal smoke and coal dust. Steam engines puffed in the distance. It was 1895. She ran up the stairs. The carpet felt brittle, worn by decades of heels. Down in the cellar, the silence was absolute. Monitors flickered on a desk. Blueprints covered the floor. It was the year 2088.

The house did not merely store time; it consumed and reshaped it. The walls breathed. Doors opened to mornings that had passed centuries ago or futures yet unwritten.

Eleanor leaned against a doorframe, watching the shadow stretch across the floorboards. She knew the answer lay not in the past, but in the control the structure exerted over existence. She closed her eyes, listening to the creak of timber settling into the new age.

She stepped back from the threshold, accepting that time bends to the will of the house.<|im_end|>
